summary: Joel Spolsky introduces a planned series on great UI design by illustrating how everyday devices have become frustratingly hard to use. He uses the simple act of turning on a cell phone as an extended example of unintuitive design — a red button that turns things on, invisible feedback, and long boot times. He argues that while desktop software UI had reached a decent standard by 2000, the explosion of consumer gadgets and the web destroyed that consistency. Despite the general decline, a few standout products like the iPod, TiVo, and Google achieved truly great design, and he promises to explore what separates merely adequate design from history-changing design.
key_points: Modern consumer gadgets have made once-simple appliances like phones and TVs harder to use through bad UI design borrowed from software
Desktop software UI had reached a reasonable standard around 2000 with consistent Mac/Windows conventions, but the web and gadget explosion undid that progress
The web lacked standard UI conventions, leading to inconsistent and confusing interaction patterns like simulated dropdown menus
A small number of exceptional products (iPod, TiVo, Google) achieved great design that made people happy despite the general trend toward incomprehensibility
There is a meaningful gap between serviceable product design and truly great design, and Joel plans to explore what makes that leap possible
money_quotes: Here's why I'm afraid to turn off my cell phone: because I can't always seem to muster the brain cells necessary to turn it back on.
It turns out it's the red button that turns it on.
In a bright room, nothing happens for six seconds. That's usually long enough to think that you've done something wrong.

It seemed like in the Olden Days you didn't have to wait for half a minute to turn on an appliance. There was a switch, up was on, you switched it, the thing went on and started spinning or shining or whatever it is that the thing was supposed to do. Instantly. End of story.
Suddenly, bad computer user interface design is seeping into the entire world.
Combined with rampant featuritis, these damn devices did more and more things but just figuring out how to do them took a degree in engineering.
A very few, very good designers were, somehow, coming up with truly great designs that were beautiful, easy to understand, fun, and which made people happy.
We're going to look at what it takes to make the leap from a servicable, decent product design to a Mindbogglingly Great, Earth-Shaking, History-Changing product design.
key_insight: While most products were becoming increasingly incomprehensible due to featuritis and inconsistent interfaces, a handful of great designs proved that making people happy through intuitive, beautiful UI was still possible — and understanding that gap is worth studying.
